title: Quiz: Quantum Materials
description: Test your understanding of topological and correlated materials

questions.question: What characterizes a topological insulator?

options.text: Bulk insulator with protected metallic surface states

questions.explanation: Topological insulators have an insulating bulk (band gap) but conducting surface states protected by time-reversal symmetry. These surface states cannot be gapped by non-magnetic perturbations.

questions.question: What is the Z₂ topological invariant?

options.text: A binary invariant (0 or 1) classifying insulators
The band gap in electron volts
The number of atoms in the unit cell

questions.explanation: The Z₂ invariant is a topological quantum number that takes only two values: ν=0 (trivial insulator) or ν=1 (topological insulator). It's calculated from the parity eigenvalues at time-reversal invariant momenta.

questions.question: What distinguishes a Weyl semimetal from a Dirac semimetal?

options.text: Weyl has broken inversion or time-reversal symmetry
Weyl has a larger band gap

questions.explanation: Weyl semimetals require broken inversion OR time-reversal symmetry to lift the degeneracy at Dirac points, creating pairs of Weyl points with opposite chirality. Dirac semimetals preserve both symmetries.

questions.question: In the McMillan-Allen-Dynes equation for Tc, what does λ represent?
questions.explanation: λ is the electron-phonon coupling constant, measuring the strength of interaction between electrons and lattice vibrations. Higher λ generally leads to higher Tc in conventional (BCS) superconductors.

questions.question: DFT alone accurately describes Mott insulators like NiO.
questions.explanation: False. Standard DFT (LDA/GGA) often predicts Mott insulators to be metals because it doesn't properly capture strong local correlations. DFT+U or DMFT is needed to open the correct insulating gap.

questions.question: Which are signatures of Weyl semimetals? (Select all)
questions.explanation: Weyl semimetals feature Fermi arcs connecting Weyl point projections on surfaces, exhibit chiral anomaly (negative magnetoresistance), and have Weyl points that act as monopoles of Berry curvature. They are gapless, not gapped.
